docs.title: Kubernetes Home Lab
docs.text:
Bare-metal Kubernetes cluster built with kubeadm on physical hardware.

This documentation describes the full lifecycle of the cluster: from hardware design to installation and operations.

docs.title: 🎯 Project Goals
docs.text:
• Understand Kubernetes internals
  • Build a production-like bare-metal cluster
  • Practice CKA/CKAD concepts
  • Maintain a fully reproducible setup

docs.title: 🏗 Architecture Summary
docs.text:
• Single control plane
  • Two worker nodes
  • Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS
  • containerd runtime
  • Calico CNI
  • Dedicated Pod CIDR (10.244.0.0/16)

docs.title: 📚 Documentation Structure
docs.text:
• Overview → Design and topology
  • Installation → Step-by-step cluster setup
  • Operations → Validation and reset procedures

docs.title: ArgoCD Exposure via Gateway
docs.text:
This section describes how ArgoCD is exposed externally using Gateway API and Cloudflare Tunnel.

The ArgoCD UI is accessible at argocd.kanismile.com without any LoadBalancer or open inbound port on the cluster.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Traffic follows the same pattern as all other services in this lab:

User → Cloudflare DNS → Cloudflare Tunnel → Gateway API → HTTPRoute → argocd-server
  • The argocd-server service remains ClusterIP
  • The NGINX Gateway Fabric handles routing via the edge-gateway
  • Cloudflare Tunnel provides secure external access

docs.title: HTTPRoute
docs.text:
The HTTPRoute is deployed in the argocd namespace and references the shared edge-gateway in the nginx-gateway namespace.

apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: HTTPRoute
metadata:
  name: argocd
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  parentRefs:
    - name: edge-gateway
      namespace: nginx-gateway
  hostnames:
    - argocd.kanismile.com
  rules:
    - matches:
        - path:
            type: PathPrefix
            value: /
      backendRefs:
        - name: argocd-server
          port: 80

Apply it with:

kubectl apply -f httproute-argocd.yaml

docs.title: Verify the Route
docs.text:
Check the HTTPRoute status:

kubectl get httproute argocd -n argocd

Expected output:

NAME     HOSTNAMES                    AGE
argocd   ["argocd.kanismile.com"]     ...

Verify it is accepted by the gateway controller:

kubectl describe httproute argocd -n argocd

Look for:

Conditions:
  Type: Accepted    Status: True
  Type: ResolvedRefs  Status: True

docs.title: Design Decisions
docs.text:
• No Ingress — Gateway API is used exclusively across the lab
  • ClusterIP preserved — the argocd-server service is not modified
  • Shared gateway — the edge-gateway is reused across all services, avoiding resource duplication
  • No TLS at the cluster level — TLS is terminated at the Cloudflare edge

Info

The edge-gateway is defined in the nginx-gateway namespace and is shared across all HTTPRoutes in the lab. See the NGINX Gateway Fabric section for details.

docs.title: ArgoCD Installation
docs.text:
This section describes how ArgoCD v3.3.0 was installed on the Kubernetes Home Lab using the official vanilla manifest.

ArgoCD is a declarative GitOps continuous delivery tool for Kubernetes. It monitors a Git repository and automatically syncs the desired state to the cluster.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• A running Kubernetes cluster
  • kubectl configured with access to the cluster
  • Sufficient permissions to create namespaces and cluster-wide resources

docs.title: Create the namespace
docs.text:
ArgoCD runs in its own dedicated namespace:

kubectl create namespace argocd

docs.title: Apply the official manifest
docs.text:
The installation uses the official upstream manifest for ArgoCD v3.3.0:

kubectl apply -n argocd -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/argoproj/argo-cd/v3.3.0/manifests/install.yaml

Note

This installs ArgoCD with all default components. No Helm chart or Kustomize overlay is used.

docs.title: Verify the installation
docs.text:
Wait for all pods to be in Running state:

kubectl get pods -n argocd

Expected output:

NAME                                                  READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
argocd-application-controller-0                       1/1     Running   0          ...
argocd-dex-server-xxxx                                1/1     Running   0          ...
argocd-notifications-controller-xxxx                  1/1     Running   0          ...
argocd-redis-xxxx                                     1/1     Running   0          ...
argocd-repo-server-xxxx                               1/1     Running   0          ...
argocd-server-xxxx                                    1/1     Running   0          ...

docs.title: Components Installed
docs.text:
Component Role argocd-server Main API server and Web UI argocd-application-controller Reconciles cluster state with Git argocd-repo-server Clones and renders Git repositories argocd-dex-server Handles SSO and authentication argocd-redis Caching layer argocd-notifications-controller Sends notifications on sync events

Info

All services are of type ClusterIP. External access is handled separately via Gateway API and Cloudflare Tunnel.

docs.title: Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Vanilla install — no Helm, no Kustomize, keeping the setup simple and auditable
  • ClusterIP only — no LoadBalancer or NodePort, external exposure is managed at the Gateway layer
  • v3.3.0 — pinned version for reproducibility

docs.title: Cloudflare Tunnel
docs.text:
This section describes how Cloudflare Tunnel is used to expose cluster services to the internet without any public IP, open port, or LoadBalancer.

All external traffic enters the cluster through a single secure outbound connection established by the cloudflared agent running inside the cluster.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
• TLS is terminated at the Cloudflare edge — traffic inside the cluster is plain HTTP
  • No inbound ports are opened on the cluster nodes
  • One tunnel handles all services via hostname-based routing

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
The cloudflared agent runs inside the cluster and maintains a persistent outbound connection to the Cloudflare edge network.

When a user requests https://argocd.kanismile.com:

  1. Cloudflare resolves the domain and routes the request through the tunnel
  2. cloudflared receives the request inside the cluster
  3. It forwards the request to edge-gateway-nginx.nginx-gateway.svc.cluster.local:80
  4. NGINX Gateway Fabric matches the hostname via the corresponding HTTPRoute
  5. The request is forwarded to the target service

docs.title: Tunnel Configuration
docs.text:
The tunnel was created via the Cloudflare Zero Trust dashboard and configured with one public hostname per service, all pointing to the same internal service:

Hostname Internal Service argocd.kanismile.com http://edge-gateway-nginx.nginx-gateway.svc.cluster.local:80 grafana.kanismile.com http://edge-gateway-nginx.nginx-gateway.svc.cluster.local:80 prometheus.kanismile.com http://edge-gateway-nginx.nginx-gateway.svc.cluster.local:80 alertmanager.kanismile.com http://edge-gateway-nginx.nginx-gateway.svc.cluster.local:80

Info

All hostnames point to the same internal URL. Hostname-based routing is handled by the HTTPRoutes at the Gateway layer — not by the tunnel itself.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
The cloudflared agent runs as a Deployment in the cloudflare namespace.

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: cloudflared
  namespace: cloudflare
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: cloudflared
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: cloudflared
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: cloudflared
          image: cloudflare/cloudflared:latest
          args:
            - tunnel
            - run
          env:
            - name: TUNNEL_TOKEN
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: cloudflared-token
                  key: TUNNEL_TOKEN
          resources:
            limits:
              memory: "128Mi"
              cpu: "100m"

Apply it with:

kubectl create namespace cloudflare
kubectl apply -f cloudflare/cloudflared.yaml

docs.title: Tunnel Token Secret
docs.text:
The tunnel token is stored as a Kubernetes Secret and injected via environment variable.

Create the secret from the token provided by the Cloudflare Zero Trust dashboard:

kubectl create secret generic cloudflared-token \
  --from-literal=TUNNEL_TOKEN=<your-tunnel-token> \
  --namespace cloudflare

Warning

Never commit the tunnel token to Git. The secret must be created manually on the cluster before applying the Deployment.

docs.title: Verify the Deployment
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -n cloudflare

Expected output:

NAME                           READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
cloudflared-xxxx               1/1     Running   0          ...

Check the tunnel is connected from the Cloudflare Zero Trust dashboard:

Zero Trust → Networks → Tunnels → k8s-homelab → Status: Healthy

docs.title: Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Single tunnel for all services — one cloudflared instance handles all hostnames
  • All routes point to the Gateway — routing logic stays in Kubernetes (HTTPRoutes), not in Cloudflare
  • Token via Secret — the tunnel token is never stored in Git
  • No public IP required — the cluster nodes have no inbound exposure

Tip

To add a new service, simply create a new HTTPRoute in the cluster and add the corresponding hostname in the Cloudflare Zero Trust dashboard pointing to http://edge-gateway-nginx.nginx-gateway.svc.cluster.local:80.

docs.title: CNI Installation (Calico)
docs.text:
This section describes how to install Calico as the Container Network Interface (CNI) for the Kubernetes Home Lab.

Calico is responsible for: - Pod-to-Pod networking across nodes - Pod IP address allocation - Network routing and encapsulation - Network policy enforcement (optional, later)

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before installing Calico, ensure that:

  • the Kubernetes control plane is initialized
  • kubectl access is configured
  • all nodes are in NotReady state (expected before CNI installation)

docs.title: Verify current node status:
docs.text:
kubectl get nodes

Expected output:

  • Control plane node is present
  • Status is NotReady

docs.title: Network design assumptions
docs.text:
This lab uses the following network ranges:

Network type CIDR Home LAN 192.168.1.0/24 Pod Network 10.244.0.0/16

docs.title: ⚠️ The Pod Network CIDR must match the value provided during kubeadm init.
Why Calico
docs.text:
Calico was selected for this home lab because it provides:

  • Native Kubernetes networking (no overlay requirement)
  • High performance and low overhead
  • Optional encapsulation (IP-in-IP or VXLAN)
  • First-class support for NetworkPolicies
  • Production-grade behavior while remaining simple to operate

docs.title: Logical Pod Network topology (Calico)
docs.text:
This diagram represents the logical networking model once Calico is installed.

  • Each node is assigned a Pod CIDR
  • Pods receive IPs from the Pod Network range
  • Calico routes Pod traffic between nodes
  • No NAT is required between Pods

docs.title: Install Calico
docs.text: Calico is installed by applying the official manifest.

docs.title: Download the Calico manifest compatible with k8s 1.34
docs.text: curl -LO https://raw.githubusercontent.com/projectcalico/calico/release-v3.31/manifests/calico.yaml

docs.title: CNI (Calico)
docs.text:
(Optional) Review the manifest:

less calico.yaml

docs.title: Apply the manifest
docs.text:
kubectl apply -f calico.yaml

This will deploy:

  • calico-node (DaemonSet)
  • calico-kube-controllers
  • required CRDs and RBAC rules

docs.title: Check Calico pods:
docs.text: kubectl get pods -n kube-system

docs.title: Expected output:
docs.text:
• calico-node pods running on all nodes
  • calico-kube-controllers running

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
calico-node-xxxxx            Running
calico-kube-controllers      Running

docs.title: Verify node readiness
docs.text:
Once Calico is running, nodes should transition to Ready.

kubectl get nodes

Expected output:

  • control plane node is Ready

docs.title: Deploy a simple test Pod:
docs.text: kubectl run test-pod --image=busybox --restart=Never -- sleep 3600

docs.title: Verify Pod status:
docs.text: kubectl get pods

docs.title: Expected:
docs.text: • Pod is Running

docs.title: (Optional) Check Pod IP:
docs.text: kubectl get pod test-pod -o wide

docs.title: Cleanup test Pod
docs.text: kubectl delete pod test-pod

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
At this stage:

  • Calico is installed and operational
  • Pod networking is functional
  • The cluster is ready to accept worker nodes

docs.title: Gateway API
docs.text:
This section describes the installation and configuration of Gateway API used to expose applications inside the Kubernetes cluster.

Gateway API provides a modern, flexible alternative to Ingress, offering a more expressive routing model and better separation of concerns.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Gateway API introduces a set of Kubernetes resources to manage traffic:

  • GatewayClass → defines the controller implementation
  • Gateway → defines entry points (listeners, ports)
  • HTTPRoute → defines routing rules

This model separates:

  • infrastructure (Gateway)
  • application routing (HTTPRoute)

docs.title: Why Gateway API
docs.text:
Gateway API was chosen over traditional Ingress because:

  • More flexible routing model
  • Better separation of responsibilities
  • Improved support for multi-team environments
  • Considered the future of Kubernetes traffic management

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Gateway API operates with:

  • a controller (NGINX Gateway Fabric)
  • a GatewayClass
  • one or more Gateways
  • multiple HTTPRoutes

GatewayClass → Gateway → HTTPRoute → Service → Pod

docs.title: GitOps Workflow
docs.text:
This section describes the GitOps workflow used in this lab to deploy and manage Kubernetes workloads via ArgoCD.

The core principle is simple: Git is the single source of truth. Any change to the cluster must go through a Git commit.

docs.title: Core Concept
docs.text:
• No manual kubectl apply for application deployments
  • ArgoCD continuously reconciles the desired state (Git) with the actual state (cluster)
  • Any drift is detected and can be corrected automatically

docs.title: 1. Define the desired state in Git
docs.text:
All Kubernetes manifests (Deployments, Services, ConfigMaps, HTTPRoutes, etc.) are stored in a Git repository.

kubernetes-home-lab/
├── apps/
│   ├── my-app/
│   │   ├── deployment.yaml
│   │   ├── service.yaml
│   │   └── httproute.yaml

docs.title: 2. Create an ArgoCD Application
docs.text:
An Application resource tells ArgoCD where to find the manifests and where to deploy them:

apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: my-app
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: default
  source:
    repoURL: https://github.com/kaninda/kubernetes-home-lab
    targetRevision: HEAD
    path: apps/my-app
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: my-app
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: true
      selfHeal: true

Apply it with:

kubectl apply -f apps/my-app/application.yaml

docs.title: 3. ArgoCD syncs the cluster
docs.text:
ArgoCD detects the new Application and reconciles the cluster state:

  • Pulls the manifests from the Git repository
  • Applies them to the target namespace
  • Reports the sync status in the UI

Tip

With automated sync policy enabled, ArgoCD will automatically apply any new commit to the target branch — no manual intervention needed.

docs.title: Sync Policies
docs.text:
Policy Behavior automated Automatically syncs on every Git change prune: true Deletes resources removed from Git selfHeal: true Reverts manual changes made directly to the cluster

Warning

With selfHeal: true, any manual kubectl apply or kubectl delete will be reverted by ArgoCD on the next reconciliation loop.

docs.title: Verify Sync Status
docs.text:
Check the application status via CLI:

kubectl get application -n argocd

Or open the ArgoCD UI at https://argocd.kanismile.com to visualize the sync status, resource tree, and live diff.

docs.title: Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Automated sync — reduces manual operations and enforces GitOps discipline
  • Self-healing — prevents configuration drift
  • Prune enabled — keeps the cluster clean when resources are removed from Git
  • Single repo — all manifests live in the same repository as the documentation

docs.title: Hardware
docs.text:
This section describes the physical hardware used to build the Kubernetes Home Lab.

The goal is to use compact, power-efficient, and affordable machines while keeping a setup close to a real-world bare-metal Kubernetes cluster.

docs.title: Hardware overview
docs.text: Node Role CPU RAM Storage Network cp-1 Control Plane Ryzen 5 3500U 16GB 512GB SSD 2.5 GbE worker-1 Worker Node Ryzen 5 5500U 32GB 500GB SSD 2.5 GbE worker-2 Worker Node Ryzen 5 5500U 32GB 500GB SSD 2.5 GbE

docs.title: Control Plane node
docs.text:
The control plane is hosted on a dedicated mini-PC.

Characteristics: - Sufficient CPU power for API server, scheduler and controller manager - Moderate memory footprint (16 GB is sufficient for a single control plane) - SSD storage for etcd and system components - Dedicated Ethernet interface

This node is intentionally not used for application workloads.

docs.title: Worker nodes
docs.text:
Worker nodes are more powerful and designed to run application workloads.

Characteristics: - Higher CPU core count for parallel workloads - More memory for pods and caches - Fast local SSD storage - Wired Ethernet connectivity

This allows realistic scheduling, resource limits and scaling experiments.

docs.title: Networking hardware
docs.text:
All nodes are connected using a dedicated 2.5 GbE Ethernet switch.

Design choices: - Wired network only (no Wi-Fi) - Predictable latency and throughput - No network bottlenecks between nodes

docs.title: Key hardware design decisions
docs.text:
• Single control plane (home lab scope)
  • Dedicated nodes (no virtualization)
  • Wired Ethernet only
  • Over-provisioned worker nodes
  • Energy-efficient hardware

docs.title: Kubernetes Installation (kubeadm - v1.34)
docs.text:
This section describes how to install Kubernetes components and initialize the cluster using kubeadm.

At the end of this section, a single-node control plane will be up and running, ready to accept worker nodes.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before proceeding, ensure that: - the operating system is prepared (see OS Preparation) - swap is disabled - containerd is running - required kernel modules and sysctl parameters are applied

All commands below must be executed as root or with sudo.

docs.title: Add Kubernetes APT repository (v1.34)
docs.text:
Install required packages

sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y apt-transport-https ca-certificates curl gpg

Create keyrings directory (if not present)

sudo mkdir -p -m 755 /etc/apt/keyrings

Add CPG key

curl -fsSL https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.34/deb/Release.key \
| sudo gpg --dearmor -o /etc/apt/keyrings/kubernetes-apt-keyring.gpg

Add Kubernetes APT repository

echo "deb [signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/kubernetes-apt-keyring.gpg] \
https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.34/deb/ /" \
| sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/kubernetes.list

Update package index

sudo apt-get update

docs.title: Install Kubernetes components (pinned version)
docs.text:
Select the Kubernetes version to install:

apt-cache madison kubeadm

Expected output:

kubeadm | 1.34.3-1.1 | https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.34/deb  Packages
kubeadm | 1.34.2-1.1 | https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.34/deb  Packages
kubeadm | 1.34.1-1.1 | https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.34/deb  Packages
kubeadm | 1.34.0-1.1 | https://pkgs.k8s.io/core:/stable:/v1.34/deb  Packages

Install kubeadm, kubelet and kubectl using the selected version (example: 1.34.3-1.1):

sudo apt-get install -y \
kubelet=1.34.3-1.1 \
kubeadm=1.34.3-1.1 \
kubectl=1.34.3-1.1

Hold package versions

Prevent automatic upgrades to avoid version drift:

sudo apt-mark hold kubelet kubeadm kubectl

docs.title: Verify installation
docs.text:
kubeadm version
kubectl version --client
kubelet --version

Expected output: - all components report v1.34.x

docs.title: Kubelet behavior before cluster initialization
docs.text:
At this stage, kubelet is installed but the cluster is not yet initialized.

It is expected that:

  • kubelet is running
  • kubelet repeatedly fails and retries
  • the node is not yet registered in the cluster

This behavior is normal and will be resolved automatically after running kubeadm init.

No manual kubelet configuration is required.

docs.title: Initialize the control plane
docs.text:
Run the following command on the control plane node only:

sudo kubeadm init \
--apiserver-advertise-address=192.168.1.53 \
--pod-network-cidr=10.244.0.0/16

The Pod CIDR must match the CNI configuration that will be installed later.

docs.title: Save the join command
docs.text:
At the end of the output, kubeadm prints a kubeadm join command.

⚠️ Save this command — it will be required to join worker nodes later.

docs.title: Configure kubectl access
docs.text:
Configure kubectl for the current user:

mkdir -p $HOME/.kube
sudo cp /etc/kubernetes/admin.conf $HOME/.kube/config
sudo chown $(id -u):$(id -g) $HOME/.kube/config

Verify access:

kubectl get nodes

docs.title: Verify control plane status
docs.text:
Check core components:

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

Expected state:

  • kube-apiserver running
  • kube-controller-manager running
  • kube-scheduler running
  • etcd running

Some pods may be in Pending state until a CNI is installed — this is normal.

docs.title: Kubernetes Reset (kubeadm)
docs.text:
This section describes how to cleanly reset a Kubernetes cluster installed with kubeadm.

A reset may be required in the following situations: - failed or incomplete cluster initialization - incorrect Pod CIDR or network configuration - CNI installation issues - certificate or kubeadm configuration errors - reinstallation from scratch

The goal is to return the system to a clean, pre-Kubernetes state without reinstalling the operating system.

docs.title: ⚠️ Important warning
docs.text:
This procedure:

  • removes all Kubernetes state
  • deletes etcd data
  • removes CNI configuration
  • does not preserve workloads or cluster data

Use it only when you intend to rebuild the cluster.

docs.title: Reset the control plane and worker nodes
docs.text: The following steps must be executed on all nodes (control plane and workers).

docs.title: Run kubeadm reset
docs.text:
sudo kubeadm reset --force

This command:

  • stops Kubernetes services
  • removes static pod manifests
  • deletes etcd data (on control plane)
  • resets kubeadm state

docs.title: Remove Kubernetes configuration files
docs.text:
rm -rf $HOME/.kube
sudo rm -rf /etc/kubernetes

docs.title: Remove CNI configuration and state:
docs.text:
sudo rm -rf /etc/cni/net.d
sudo rm -rf /var/lib/cni

docs.title: If Calico was used, also remove its state:
docs.text: sudo rm -rf /var/lib/calico

docs.title: Cleanup iptables rules (recommended)
docs.text:
Kubernetes and CNI plugins modify iptables rules. Reset them to avoid conflicts.

sudo iptables -F
sudo iptables -t nat -F
sudo iptables -t mangle -F
sudo iptables -X

docs.title: Restart container runtime and kubelet
docs.text:
sudo systemctl restart containerd
sudo systemctl restart kubelet

docs.title: Ensure Kubernetes is no longer active:
docs.text:
kubectl get nodes

Expected:

  • command fails or reports no configuration

Check that no Kubernetes ports are listening:

sudo ss -lntp | grep 6443

Expected:

  • no output

docs.title: Optional: reboot the node
docs.text:
A reboot ensures:

  • clean network state
  • cleared virtual interfaces
  • clean kernel routing tables
sudo reboot

docs.title: Logical Architecture
docs.text:
This section describes the logical architecture of the Kubernetes Home Lab, focusing on traffic flow, component responsibilities, and design decisions.

Unlike the physical topology, this view focuses on how components interact to expose services from inside the cluster to external users.

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
The platform is designed to expose applications securely without requiring public inbound connectivity.

Instead of exposing services via public IPs or LoadBalancers, this architecture relies on Cloudflare Tunnel and Gateway API.

docs.title: High-Level Flow
docs.text:
External traffic follows this path:

User → Cloudflare DNS → Cloudflare Tunnel → Gateway API → HTTPRoute → Service → Pod

docs.title: Cloudflare DNS
docs.text:
• Public entry point
  • Resolves application domain (e.g. argocd.example.com)
  • Managed externally via Cloudflare

docs.title: Cloudflare Tunnel (cloudflared)
docs.text:
• Runs inside the cluster as a Deployment
  • Establishes outbound-only connections to Cloudflare
  • Eliminates the need for:
    • public IP addresses
    • port forwarding
    • firewall exposure

➡️ This significantly improves security by avoiding inbound traffic.

docs.title: Gateway API
docs.text:
Gateway API provides Kubernetes-native traffic routing.

It replaces traditional Ingress with a more flexible model.

Main resources:

  • GatewayClass → defines the controller
  • Gateway → entry point (listeners, ports)
  • HTTPRoute → routing rules

docs.title: NGINX Gateway Fabric
docs.text:
• Implements Gateway API
  • Acts as the data plane
  • Routes HTTP traffic to services

Responsibilities:

  • TLS termination (optional)
  • Host-based routing
  • Path-based routing

docs.title: HTTPRoute
docs.text:
Defines how traffic is routed:

Example:

hostnames:
  - argocd.example.com

Routes requests to: - Service: argocd-server - Port: 80

docs.title: Services
docs.text:
• Expose Kubernetes workloads internally
  • Usually of type ClusterIP
  • Gateway handles external access

docs.title: Pods
docs.text:
• Actual application workloads
  • Example: ArgoCD server

docs.title: Traffic Flow (Detailed)
docs.text:
User → Cloudflare DNS → Cloudflare Tunnel → Gateway API → HTTPRoute → Service → Pod

  1. The user sends a request to: https://argocd.example.com

  2. The domain is resolved by Cloudflare DNS

  3. The request is routed through the Cloudflare edge network

  4. Cloudflare forwards the request to the Cloudflare Tunnel

  5. The cloudflared agent (running in the cluster) receives the request

  6. The request is forwarded to the Gateway API listener

  7. The HTTPRoute matches the hostname

  8. The request is forwarded to the corresponding Service

  9. The Service forwards traffic to the Pod

docs.title: No LoadBalancer
docs.text:
This lab does not rely on cloud LoadBalancers or MetalLB.

External exposure is handled via Cloudflare Tunnel.

docs.title: Gateway API instead of Ingress
docs.text:
Gateway API is used instead of traditional Ingress because:

  • More expressive routing model
  • Better separation of concerns
  • Future Kubernetes standard

docs.title: Outbound-only connectivity
docs.text:
No inbound ports are opened on the network.

All connections are initiated from inside the cluster via cloudflared.

This improves security by reducing the attack surface.

docs.title: Separation of concerns
docs.text: Layer Responsibility Cloudflare DNS + Edge routing cloudflared Secure tunnel Gateway API Routing model NGINX Gateway Traffic processing Kubernetes Workload execution

docs.title: Security Model
docs.text:
• No public IP exposure on cluster nodes
  • No open ports on the router
  • All traffic is initiated from inside the cluster
  • TLS is terminated at Cloudflare edge (optional)

This model significantly reduces the attack surface compared to traditional setups.

docs.title: Network Topology
docs.text:
This section describes the physical and logical network topology of the Kubernetes Home Lab.

The goal is to clearly understand: - how nodes are connected at the network level - how Kubernetes components communicate - how Pod networking is handled

docs.title: High-level overview
docs.text:
The cluster is deployed on a home LAN with: - a router providing DHCP reservations - a dedicated 2.5 GbE switch - all Kubernetes nodes connected via Ethernet

All nodes use static IPs or DHCP reservations to ensure predictable addressing.

docs.title: Kubernetes control plane communication
docs.text:
• The kube-apiserver runs on the control plane node
  • Worker nodes communicate exclusively with the API server
  • All control traffic uses the LAN network

docs.title: Pod network (Calico)
docs.text: The cluster uses Calico as the CNI. - Each node is assigned a Pod CIDR - Pods communicate directly across nodes - Pod traffic is encapsulated and routed by Calico - The Pod CIDR does not overlap with the LAN network

docs.title: NGINX Gateway Fabric
docs.text:
This section describes how NGINX Gateway Fabric v2.4.1 was installed and configured as the Gateway API implementation in this lab.

NGINX Gateway Fabric acts as the data plane for the Gateway API. It translates Gateway and HTTPRoute resources into NGINX routing configuration, and handles all inbound HTTP traffic coming from the Cloudflare Tunnel.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
Two deployments run in the nginx-gateway namespace:

Component Image Role nginx-gateway ghcr.io/nginx/nginx-gateway-fabric:2.4.1 Controller — watches Gateway API resources edge-gateway-nginx ghcr.io/nginx/nginx-gateway-fabric/nginx:2.4.1 Data plane — handles HTTP traffic

The controller continuously reconciles GatewayClass, Gateway, and HTTPRoute resources and configures the NGINX data plane accordingly.

docs.title: Installation
docs.text: NGINX Gateway Fabric is installed using the official upstream manifests (no Helm).

docs.title: 1. Install the Gateway API CRDs
docs.text: kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/gateway-api/releases/download/v1.2.0/standard-install.yaml

docs.title: 2. Install NGINX Gateway Fabric
docs.text:
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nginx/nginx-gateway-fabric/v2.4.1/deploy/crds.yaml
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nginx/nginx-gateway-fabric/v2.4.1/deploy/default/deploy.yaml

Note

This installs NGINX Gateway Fabric in the nginx-gateway namespace with all default settings.

docs.title: 3. Verify the installation
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -n nginx-gateway

Expected output:

NAME                                  READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
nginx-gateway-xxxx                    1/1     Running   0          ...
edge-gateway-nginx-xxxx               1/1     Running   0          ...

docs.title: GatewayClass
docs.text:
The GatewayClass resource registers NGINX Gateway Fabric as a Gateway API controller:

kubectl get gatewayclass

Expected output:

NAME    CONTROLLER                                   ACCEPTED   AGE
nginx   gateway.nginx.org/nginx-gateway-controller   True       ...

The GatewayClass is automatically created by the install manifest and links to the nginx-gateway-proxy-config NginxProxy configuration.

docs.title: Gateway
docs.text:
A single shared Gateway named edge-gateway is deployed in the nginx-gateway namespace. It serves as the unique entry point for all HTTP traffic in the lab.

apiVersion: gateway.networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Gateway
metadata:
  name: edge-gateway
  namespace: nginx-gateway
spec:
  gatewayClassName: nginx
  listeners:
    - name: http
      protocol: HTTP
      port: 80
      allowedRoutes:
        namespaces:
          from: All

Apply it with:

kubectl apply -f nginx_gateway_fabric/gateway.yaml

Key design choices:

  • Single listener on port 80 (HTTP)
  • from: All — accepts HTTPRoutes from any namespace in the cluster
  • Shared gateway — all services reuse this single entry point

docs.title: Service Exposure
docs.text:
The edge-gateway-nginx service is exposed as a NodePort:

edge-gateway-nginx   NodePort   10.103.92.37   80:31224/TCP

The Cloudflare Tunnel (cloudflared) forwards traffic to this service using its internal DNS name:

http://edge-gateway-nginx.nginx-gateway.svc.cluster.local:80

Info

No LoadBalancer or public IP is required. The NodePort is only reachable internally — Cloudflare Tunnel handles the secure external connection.

docs.title: Verify the Gateway
docs.text:
Check the Gateway status:

kubectl get gateway edge-gateway -n nginx-gateway

Check attached routes:

kubectl get httproute -A

Expected — 4 routes currently attached to the edge-gateway:

NAMESPACE    NAME           HOSTNAMES                        AGE
argocd       argocd         ["argocd.kanismile.com"]         ...
monitoring   grafana        ["grafana.kanismile.com"]        ...
monitoring   prometheus     ["prometheus.kanismile.com"]     ...
monitoring   alertmanager   ["alertmanager.kanismile.com"]   ...

docs.title: Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Manifest install over Helm — keeps the setup explicit and auditable
  • Single shared Gateway — avoids duplicating listener config per service
  • NodePort over LoadBalancer — no cloud provider or MetalLB required
  • from: All — allows HTTPRoutes to be defined close to each application's namespace

docs.title: OS Preparation (Ubuntu Server)
docs.text:
This section describes all system-level prerequisites required before installing Kubernetes using kubeadm.

The goal is to ensure:

  • system stability
  • Kubernetes compatibility
  • predictable and reproducible behavior across all nodes

All steps below must be applied on every node:

  • control plane
  • worker nodes

docs.title: Base assumptions
docs.text:
• OS: Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS (or newer)
  • Architecture: amd64
  • Network: static IP or DHCP reservation
  • User has sudo privileges

docs.title: Disable swap
docs.text: Kubernetes requires swap to be disabled. The kubelet will refuse to start if swap is enabled.

docs.title: Disable swap immediately
docs.text: sudo swapoff -a

docs.title: Disable swap permanently
docs.text:
Edit /etc/fstab and comment out any swap entry:

sudo sed -i '/ swap / s/^/#/' /etc/fstab

docs.title: Verify swap status
docs.text:
free -h

Expected output: Swap: 0B

swapon --show

Expected no output

docs.title: Load required kernel modules
docs.text: Kubernetes networking requires specific kernel modules.

docs.title: Configure modules to load at boot
docs.text:
sudo tee /etc/modules-load.d/k8s.conf <<EOF
overlay
br_netfilter
EOF

docs.title: Load modules immediately
docs.text:
sudo modprobe overlay
sudo modprobe br_netfilter

docs.title: Verify loaded modules
docs.text: lsmod | grep -E 'overlay|br_netfilter'

docs.title: Configure kernel parameters (sysctl)
docs.text: Kubernetes requires proper packet forwarding and bridge traffic handling.

docs.title: Configure sysctl parameters
docs.text:
sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/k8s.conf <<EOF
net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-iptables  = 1
net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-ip6tables = 1
net.ipv4.ip_forward                = 1
EOF

docs.title: Apply settings
docs.text: sudo sysctl --system

docs.title: Verify settings
docs.text:
sysctl net.bridge.bridge-nf-call-iptables
sysctl net.ipv4.ip_forward

Expected value: 1

docs.title: Install container runtime (containerd)
docs.text: Kubernetes requires a CRI-compatible container runtime. This lab uses containerd.

docs.title: Install containerd
docs.text:
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y containerd

docs.title: Configure containerd
docs.text:
sudo mkdir -p /etc/containerd
containerd config default | sudo tee /etc/containerd/config.toml

docs.title: Enable systemd cgroup driver
docs.text:
Edit /etc/containerd/config.toml and ensure:

SystemdCgroup = true

You can do it automatically:

sudo sed -i 's/SystemdCgroup = false/SystemdCgroup = true/' /etc/containerd/config.toml

docs.title: Restart and enable containerd
docs.text:
sudo systemctl restart containerd
sudo systemctl enable containerd

docs.title: Verify containerd status
docs.text: systemctl status containerd

docs.title: Verify system readiness
docs.text:
Before moving forward, ensure the following: - swap is disabled - required kernel modules are loaded - sysctl parameters are applied - containerd is running

This node is now ready for Kubernetes installation using kubeadm.

docs.title: Network ranges
docs.text: Network type CIDR Home LAN 192.168.1.0/24 Pod Network 10.244.0.0/16

docs.title: Key design decisions
docs.text:
• Wired Ethernet only for cluster nodes
  • Static addressing for predictability
  • Dedicated Pod CIDR to avoid routing conflicts
  • Single control plane (home lab scope)

This topology provides a simple, stable and production-like foundation for Kubernetes experimentation.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This documentation describes the design, setup, and operation of a bare-metal Kubernetes home lab.

The cluster is built using kubeadm on Ubuntu Server, with a strong focus on: - clarity - reproducibility - production-like behavior

While this is a home lab, all design choices aim to stay as close as possible to real-world Kubernetes environments.

docs.title: Cluster goals
docs.text:
The main goals of this Kubernetes Home Lab are:

  • Learn Kubernetes fundamentals on bare metal
  • Understand cluster internals and networking
  • Practice installation, upgrades and troubleshooting
  • Experiment with CNI, storage and GitOps tools
  • Maintain a simple but realistic setup

docs.title: High-level architecture
docs.text:
The cluster consists of: - one dedicated control plane node - multiple worker nodes - a flat Layer 2 home LAN - static IP addressing or DHCP reservations - a dedicated Pod network

All nodes run: - Ubuntu Server - containerd as container runtime - kubeadm-managed Kubernetes components

docs.title: Design principles
docs.text:
This cluster follows a few key design principles:

  • Simplicity over complexity
  • Predictability over automation
  • Reproducibility over convenience
  • Documentation-first approach

Each component is installed and configured explicitly to ensure full understanding of what happens under the hood.

docs.title: Scope and limitations
docs.text:
This home lab intentionally has the following limitations:

  • Single control plane (no HA)
  • No external load balancer
  • No cloud provider integration
  • No managed services

These limitations keep the cluster easy to reason about while still allowing meaningful Kubernetes experimentation.

docs.title: Documentation structure
docs.text:
The documentation is organized as follows:

  1. Hardware description reminds the physical foundation
  2. Network topology explains physical and logical connectivity
  3. OS preparation ensures all nodes are Kubernetes-ready
  4. Kubernetes installation describes kubeadm setup
  5. CNI configuration focuses on pod networking
  6. Validation ensures cluster health and correctness

Each section builds on the previous one and should be followed in order.

docs.title: Cluster Validation
docs.text:
This section validates that the Kubernetes cluster is healthy, functional and ready for workloads.

The checks below are intentionally simple and reproducible. They focus on core Kubernetes components, networking, and basic scheduling.

docs.title: Node status
docs.text:
Verify that all nodes are registered and ready:

kubectl get nodes -o wide

docs.title: Expected:
docs.text:
• all nodes are in Ready state
  • control plane has the role control-plane
  • worker nodes have no role
  • correct internal IPs are reported

docs.title: Check core system components:
docs.text: kubectl get pods -n kube-system

docs.title: Expected:
docs.text:
• kube-apiserver: Running
  • kube-controller-manager: Running
  • kube-scheduler: Running
  • etcd: Running
  • calico-node: Running on all nodes
  • calico-kube-controllers: Running

No pods should be in CrashLoopBackOff or Pending.

docs.title: Verify control plane component health:
docs.text:
kubectl get componentstatuses

Expected:

  • all components report Healthy

Note: This command is deprecated but still useful for quick validation.

docs.title: Cluster events
docs.text:
Inspect recent cluster events:

kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp

docs.title: Expected:
docs.text:
• no recurring errors
  • no certificate or networking failures

Warnings may appear briefly during startup but should not persist.

docs.title: Validate CoreDNS functionality:
docs.text:
kubectl run dns-test \
--image=busybox \
--restart=Never \
--rm -it -- \
nslookup kubernetes.default

docs.title: Expected:
docs.text:
• DNS resolution succeeds
  • service IP is returned

docs.title: Deploy two test pods on different nodes:
docs.text:
kubectl run pod-a --image=busybox --restart=Never -- sleep 3600
kubectl run pod-b --image=busybox --restart=Never -- sleep 3600

docs.title: Check pod placement and IPs:
docs.text: kubectl get pods -o wide

docs.title: Verify connectivity:
docs.text: kubectl exec pod-a -- ping -c 3 <POD-B-IP>

docs.title: Expected:
docs.text:
• ICMP traffic succeeds
  • no packet loss

docs.title: Cleanup test pods
docs.text: kubectl delete pod pod-a pod-b

docs.title: Verify API access and permissions:
docs.text: kubectl auth can-i get pods --all-namespaces

docs.title: Expected:
docs.text: • yes

docs.title: At this stage:
docs.text:
• All nodes are Ready
  • Core control plane components are healthy
  • Calico networking is functional
  • DNS resolution works
  • Pod-to-Pod communication is verified

The Kubernetes cluster is fully operational and ready for workloads.

docs.title: Join Worker Nodes
docs.text:
This section describes how to join worker nodes to the Kubernetes cluster using kubeadm join.

At the end of this section: - all worker nodes will be part of the cluster - nodes will be in Ready state - Pod networking will work across nodes

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before joining worker nodes, ensure that:

  • Kubernetes control plane is initialized
  • Calico CNI is installed and running
  • kubeadm join command is available
  • Worker nodes completed OS Preparation
  • Kubernetes packages are installed on worker nodes

Verify cluster status on the control plane:

kubectl get nodes

Expected output:

  • control plane node is Ready

docs.title: Retrieve the join command
docs.text:
On the control plane node, retrieve (or regenerate) the join command:

sudo kubeadm token create --print-join-command

docs.title: Example output:
docs.text:
kubeadm join 192.168.1.53:6443 \
--token abcdef.0123456789abcdef \
--discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx

docs.title: ⚠️ This command is unique and time-limited.
Execute on each worker node
docs.text:
Run the kubeadm join command on each worker node only:

sudo kubeadm join 192.168.1.53:6443 \
--token abcdef.0123456789abcdef \
--discovery-token-ca-cert-hash sha256:xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx

docs.title: This command:
docs.text:
• registers the node with the API server
  • installs required certificates
  • starts kubelet on the worker node

docs.title: Verify node registration
docs.text:
On the control plane, verify node status:

kubectl get nodes

Expected output:

  • control plane node: Ready
  • worker nodes: Ready

docs.title: Example:
docs.text:
NAME        STATUS   ROLES           AGE     VERSION
cp-1        Ready    control-plane   20m     v1.34.x
worker-1    Ready    <none>           2m     v1.34.x
worker-2    Ready    <none>           2m     v1.34.x

docs.title: Deploy a test Pod:
docs.text: kubectl run nginx-test --image=nginx --restart=Never

docs.title: Check where the Pod is scheduled:
docs.text: kubectl get pod nginx-test -o wide

docs.title: Expected:
docs.text:
• Pod is Running
  • Pod is scheduled on a worker node
  • Pod has an IP from the Pod CIDR range

docs.title: Cleanup test Pod
docs.text: kubectl delete pod nginx-test

docs.title: Node stays in NotReady
docs.text:
Check CNI status:

kubectl get pods -n kube-system

Ensure:

  • calico-node is running on the worker node
  • no CrashLoopBackOff

docs.title: Generate a new token:
docs.text: sudo kubeadm token create --print-join-command

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
At this stage:

  • all nodes are successfully joined
  • Pod networking works across nodes
  • the Kubernetes cluster is fully operational
